aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This is a dark workplace comedy about AI, art, labor, and moral compromise. The novel's risks are not gratuitous — they emerge from the premise's pressures. Lean into: the specific humiliation of having your creative work rated by strangers on a five-point scale; the moral vertigo of befriending people you plan to betray; the comedy of a painter who is terrible at coding but too angry to quit; the economic desperation that makes gig work feel like a lifeline even when it is degrading; the creeping horror of watching your own vocabulary get overwritten by corporate evaluation language. Do not retreat from any of these. The biggest creative risk in this novel is not a single shocking scene but the sustained commitment to showing how a system designed to evaluate art eventually evaluates the evaluator — and finding that funny and devastating in the same breath.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism for difficult content — economic shame, professional humiliation, moral compromise, grief, attraction, anger — is a craft failure equal to bad prose. When Nick's poverty is the subject, the prose must name amounts, count coins, and show the specific texture of not having enough. When Nick's sabotage causes harm, the prose must not retreat into abstraction. When characters are attracted to each other, the prose must inhabit the attraction physically and specifically. The novel's comedy does not exempt it from emotional specificity; if anything, the comic register demands more precision because the reader needs to feel the real thing beneath the joke.

chapter_spine.conflict: economic displacement — Nick's last client replaces him with AI
chapter_spine.consequence: Working artist with one client becomes displaced artist with a delusional revenge plan
must_land_beats: Nick's financial desperation and the specifics of his Oakland life
The Zoom call where the publisher drops him for Midjourney
Nick's painter's eye established through how he sees and describes images
The plan crystallizes by dawn — learn to code, infiltrate, poison the data
chapter_spine.pressure_source: economic displacement — Nick's last client replaces him with AI
secondary_character_beats: Jess Tavares exists as a phone contact or text presence — Nick's last link to the art world, still painting, still broke

setups_to_plant.description: Nick's trained visual intelligence — how he sees anatomy, light, composition — must register as central to his character, not as a plot convenience
Nick's unsold work from his last gallery show exists online, scrapable. Mention the gallery or the digital presence of his portfolio without flagging it as plot-relevant
Nick's specific poverty — overdue rent, cereal for dinner, paint supplies vs. groceries — establishes that the labeling job will become an economic lifeline, not just a cover

chapter_spine.state_shift: Working artist with one client becomes displaced artist with a delusional revenge plan

chapter_spine.conflict: Nick's total incompetence at programming colliding with his stubborn refusal to quit
chapter_spine.consequence: Vague revenge idea becomes committed delusional action — Nick now has just enough dangerous knowledge to be plausible
must_land_beats: The specific comedy of a painter trying to learn Python — his visual brain vs. boolean logic
Nick memorizes solution patterns without understanding them, like memorizing color swatches
The loneliness of the project — Christmas alone, ramen, LeetCode
Nick applies to Canopy AI by late January
chapter_spine.pressure_source: Nick's total incompetence at programming colliding with his stubborn refusal to quit
secondary_character_beats: Jess calls from her residency — she's still making art, still broke but working. Nick doesn't tell her the plan. The contrast between her honest artistic struggle and his covert project should register.

chapter_spine.conflict: Nick must hide his incompetence at coding while showcasing his visual expertise — one will get him caught, the other will get him hired
chapter_spine.consequence: Outsider with a plan becomes an infiltrator with a badge — Nick is inside Canopy
must_land_beats: The coding portion as controlled disaster — memorized patterns barely holding
The image evaluation portion as revelation — Nick spots errors the answer key misses
The interviewer's confusion at the disconnect between coding weakness and visual brilliance
Nick sitting in the parking lot afterward, hands shaking

payoffs_to_land.description: Nick's trained visual intelligence pays off spectacularly in the evaluation section, catching anatomical and lighting errors no other candidate sees

chapter_spine.pressure_source: Nick must hide his incompetence at coding while showcasing his visual expertise — one will get him caught, the other will get him hired

chapter_spine.conflict: Nick enters a world he expected to despise and finds it populated by people like him
chapter_spine.consequence: Saboteur enters the building and discovers the human landscape he will have to betray
must_land_beats: The physical reality of the labeling floor — fluorescent lights, beige carpet, rows of monitors, the murmur of rating
Introduction of all four key coworkers: Dani, Tommy, Bao, Priya — each must register as a distinct person, not a type
The evaluation rubric and its categories: prompt adherence, anatomical accuracy, lighting consistency, compositional balance, stylistic coherence
Nick's realization that his coworkers are mostly artists
chapter_spine.pressure_source: Nick enters a world he expected to despise and finds it populated by people like him

chapter_spine.conflict: Nick's genuine competence creates a promotion track that serves his sabotage but also binds him deeper into a workplace he was supposed to despise
chapter_spine.consequence: Nick's competence is real, making the contradiction of his mission tangible; his coworkers' personal stakes become specific
must_land_beats: Nick's evaluations flagged as exceptional — his ratings align with expert consensus better than anyone on the floor
Nick's social incompetence at corporate culture contrasted with his visual brilliance
Dani's custody situation surfacing through overheard phone calls
Promotion to senior evaluator — each step up gives Nick more access to the system he plans to break
chapter_spine.pressure_source: Nick's genuine competence creates a promotion track that serves his sabotage but also binds him deeper into a workplace he was supposed to despise
secondary_character_beats: Dani helping Nick navigate Slack and corporate language — competent, slightly amused, with the guardedness of someone who can't afford workplace drama
Priya recognizing Nick's talent and promoting him — her genuine investment in quality, not just metrics

setups_to_plant.description: Dani's custody battle over Luna — this job is the 'stable employment' on her court filing. Establish through fragments, not exposition: a stairwell phone call, her voice tight and controlled
Priya's stress manifesting physically — early signs like antacids, tension, exhaustion she hides. Do not overdramatize; just let it be present

chapter_spine.state_shift: Nick's competence is real, making the contradiction of his mission tangible; his coworkers' personal stakes become specific

chapter_spine.consequence: Relational — Nick's bond with Bao becomes a genuine complication, not an abstract one

Bao and Nick bonding over color theory and shared visual language
Bao's wife Mai and her early-onset dementia — told matter-of-factly, not sentimentally
Nick riding BART home at 2 AM, unsettled by his own reflection
chapter_spine.pressure_source: Nick's need to map the data pipeline for sabotage puts him in intimate proximity with Bao, whose humanity complicates everything

setups_to_plant.description: Bao's deliberate evaluation pace earns performance warnings. His slowness is quality, but the system reads it as deficiency. This must be seeded here or in Ch 7 so his firing in Ch 17 feels structurally inevitable, not arbitrary

chapter_spine.state_shift: Relational — Nick's bond with Bao becomes a genuine complication, not an abstract one

chapter_spine.consequence: External institutional pressure mounts; the system's hypocrisy becomes visible; Tommy's AI art prizes confront Nick with a funhouse version of his own project

Tommy's revelation about submitting AI art to competitions under a pseudonym — and winning
Nick unable to articulate his objection without exposing his own plans
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The AI arms race squeezes the evaluation floor from above while Tommy's confession confronts Nick with a mirror of his own logic

Tommy's ironic detachment revealed as something more desperate than cool — the student loans, the unfinished degree, the need for this joke to also be an income

The painting metaphor — bad pigment mixed into primer, invisible until the wall blooms
The realization that guardrails catch random noise and laziness, not systematic expert-level sabotage

chapter_spine.pressure_source: Marcus offers Nick the calibration-team position — exactly what Nick needs — while Dani is passed over for the same role

must_land_beats: Nick's disorientation at having power over the entire rating system — a few dozen calibration anchors shape models used by millions
The first real sabotage runs: systematic, small, individually defensible misratings
Nick's private records kept in a physical notebook, pen on paper, nothing digital
A training batch containing photos from Nick's former gallery — not his paintings yet, but the gallery walls, the lighting, a price tag visible in one corner

chapter_spine.conflict: Nick's creative identity has been colonized by evaluation language; his attempt to paint fails; his withdrawal from Dani is a consequence of the lie he lives inside
chapter_spine.consequence: Nick's original self is becoming unrecognizable; his retreat from Dani is not noble restraint but the cost of sustained deception
must_land_beats: Nick's internal language colonized by rubric terms — he can't look at winter light without scoring it
The failed painting attempt: technically proficient, rubric-compliant, dead
Luna's holiday recital — the stumbling performance, the hot gym, Luna's horse drawing as the most alive thing Nick has seen in months
The almost-intimacy with Dani: a kiss in the kitchen, his retreat, his inability to be honest

setups_to_plant.description: Nick pulls away from Dani not from lack of desire but because closeness requires honesty he has made himself incapable of. This withdrawal pattern seeds his inability to confess in Ch 18

chapter_spine.state_shift: Nick's original self is becoming unrecognizable; his retreat from Dani is not noble restraint but the cost of sustained deception

chapter_spine.conflict: Automated QA flags anomalies in calibration data; a company audit convenes; the investigation lands on a scapegoat in Manila instead of Nick
chapter_spine.consequence: Near-exposure survived, but the Manila scapegoating previews what will happen to Nick's own coworkers when the real damage surfaces; Nick doubles down
must_land_beats: The automated flags — the system can detect problems but can't identify systematic expert-level corruption
Marcus convening the audit — his genuine concern for quality integrity
The Manila scapegoat — real workers fired for a real problem that conveniently masks Nick's sabotage
Nick's decision to accelerate rather than abort

payoffs_to_land.description: Bao's slow evaluation pace, flagged earlier, makes him vulnerable in any audit — this registered concern carries into Ch 17's firing

chapter_spine.pressure_source: Automated QA flags anomalies in calibration data; a company audit convenes; the investigation lands on a scapegoat in Manila instead of Nick
secondary_character_beats: The Manila workers should register as real casualties, not just plot mechanics — they were gaming completion bonuses because the per-task pay is too low, which is the same economic pressure that puts everyone on the San Leandro floor

setups_to_plant.description: The corporate investigation pattern established here — blunt, impersonal, focused on metrics rather than intent — is the same machinery that will grind through the San Leandro team in Ch 17

chapter_spine.state_shift: Near-exposure survived, but the Manila scapegoating previews what will happen to Nick's own coworkers when the real damage surfaces; Nick doubles down

chapter_spine.conflict: Nick must maintain flawless surface performance while systematically corrupting the calibration pipeline over three weeks
chapter_spine.consequence: Sabotage complete and irreversible — the corrupted data is in the pipeline with no mechanism for recall
must_land_beats: The methodical three-week execution: rating correctly most of the time, poisoning specific categories strategically
The double consciousness of correct-and-corrupt — the exhaustion of maintaining two simultaneous evaluation standards
The specific corruption: biasing calibration anchors toward anatomical distortion, impossible lighting, uncanny-valley faces
Completion: pulling over on the 880 shoulder, throwing up, lying in bed unable to name the feeling

payoffs_to_land.description: The method discovered in Ch 8 and accessed in Ch 10 is now fully executed — systematic corruption of calibration anchors across the flagship pipeline

chapter_spine.pressure_source: Nick must maintain flawless surface performance while systematically corrupting the calibration pipeline over three weeks

chapter_spine.conflict: Nick's sabotage produces viral content instead of discrediting AI — his revenge is absorbed and aestheticized by the culture
chapter_spine.consequence: Nick's revenge produces the opposite of justice; the grotesqueries become a cultural phenomenon more famous than his paintings ever were
must_land_beats: The corrupted models producing six-fingered hands, melted faces, impossible anatomy
The public response: not horror but delight — memes, merchandise, a Brooklyn art collective, think pieces about 'digital grotesque'
Tommy texting the group chat that the glitched images 'go harder than anything I saw in art school'
Nick alone in his apartment, surrounded by unsold paintings nobody rated higher than a 4, laughing until he stops

payoffs_to_land.description: Tommy's comfort with AI aesthetics (seeded in Ch 7) makes his embrace of the viral glitch outputs feel character-consistent, not plot-convenient

chapter_spine.pressure_source: Nick's sabotage produces viral content instead of discrediting AI — his revenge is absorbed and aestheticized by the culture
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nick's revenge produces the opposite of justice; the grotesqueries become a cultural phenomenon more famous than his paintings ever were

chapter_spine.conflict: The internal investigation reaches Nick's coworkers — every person he cares about pays for what he did
chapter_spine.consequence: The human cost of the sabotage lands on the people Nick was supposed to be protecting; his moral position becomes indefensible by any logic including his own
must_land_beats: Bao fired — clearing his desk with quiet dignity, shaking hands with each person
Dani's position threatened — her custody lawyer's call, the employment interruption as legal ammunition
Tommy on probation for pre-existing quality deviations swept into the audit
Priya's panic attack in the bathroom — Nick hearing it through the wall

payoffs_to_land.description: Dani's custody vulnerability, seeded in Ch 5, now faces a direct threat from the job instability Nick caused
Bao's slow evaluation pace, flagged in Ch 6, makes him the easiest target in the purge — his quality is indistinguishable from deficiency in the audit's metrics
The blunt, metrics-driven investigation pattern from Ch 14 repeats at San Leandro — same corporate machinery, now grinding through Nick's friends
Priya's accumulating stress, seeded from Ch 5/7, culminates in a panic attack

chapter_spine.pressure_source: The internal investigation reaches Nick's coworkers — every person he cares about pays for what he did
secondary_character_beats: Each person's suffering must connect to their independent pressure established earlier: Bao's firing hits his wife's care; Dani's job threat hits custody; Tommy's probation hits his loan payments; Priya's breakdown echoes the one that ended her teaching career
Nick helping carry Bao's box to his car — the specific physical act of assisting in the consequence of his own crime
chapter_spine.state_shift: The human cost of the sabotage lands on the people Nick was supposed to be protecting; his moral position becomes indefensible by any logic including his own

chapter_spine.conflict: Nick faces three natural openings to confess and chooses silence each time; the industry responds to his sabotage by hiring more labelers
chapter_spine.consequence: Nick's silence becomes self-destruction — his relationships, his integrity, and his identity as an artist are all casualties; the system absorbs sabotage the way it absorbs art, by rating it and moving on
must_land_beats: Three almost-confessions: to Dani (clearing Bao's desk), to Priya (her near-resignation), to Marcus (direct question about pipeline anomalies)
Canopy announces hiring 500 additional labelers to rebuild corrupted data — Nick's sabotage strengthened the human layer
The final scene: Nick alone, paint and canvas, staring at the blank surface, reaching for the laptop instead, logging in, opening a new batch, starting to rate

payoffs_to_land.description: Nick's withdrawal from intimacy in Ch 13 repeats as withdrawal from confession — the same incapacity for honesty, now with higher stakes
The industry's pattern of scaling the human layer under pressure (seeded Ch 7) produces the ironic final outcome: sabotage causes MORE hiring, strengthening the system

chapter_spine.pressure_source: Nick faces three natural openings to confess and chooses silence each time; the industry responds to his sabotage by hiring more labelers
secondary_character_beats: Dani's grief for Bao — cleaning out his desk station, finding his handwritten evaluation notes with too-detailed color observations
Priya telling Nick she's thinking of quitting, her eyes red — the human cost of management pressure, now compounded by the investigation
Marcus asking Nick directly whether he has 'noticed anything anomalous' — the question as an offered lifeline Nick refuses to take
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nick's silence becomes self-destruction — his relationships, his integrity, and his identity as an artist are all casualties; the system absorbs sabotage the way it absorbs art, by rating it and moving on

character_state_table.evasion_style: Changes the subject through humor or by offering a specific observation about something in the physical environment. When cornered about something personal, he answers a slightly different question than the one asked. When cornered about the sabotage, he goes very still and very literal.
character_state_table.indirectness: Highly indirect about his own emotional states. Will describe a situation, a physical sensation, or someone else's behavior rather than naming what he feels. More direct about other people's problems than his own.
character_state_table.interruption_habit: Tends to let others finish, then redirect rather than rebut. When he does interrupt, it is abrupt and topic-shifting — he cuts across a conversation rather than competing within it. Rarely fights for the floor.
character_state_table.private_register: Angry, obsessive, increasingly colonized by evaluation language. Interior monologue shifts across the novel: early chapters use painterly vocabulary (color, light, composition); middle chapters bleed rubric language into his perception; late chapters are almost entirely metric-infected. The corruption of his internal voice is the novel's primary psychological arc.
character_state_table.public_register: Casual, self-deprecating, quick wit that deflects rather than connects. Uses humor to avoid sincerity. Can be charming in groups but deflects one-on-one vulnerability. Defaults to understatement about his own situation.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: Low in speech — he avoids saying the same thing twice because repetition feels like exposure. In thought, moderate — he circles the same anxieties but from different angles.
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Frequent in private thought, rare in speech. He second-guesses himself internally but presents a more settled front to others. When he does self-correct aloud, it usually involves walking back a claim or softening an opinion he meant more strongly than he said.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Mostly completes sentences in casual conversation. Under emotional pressure, trails off or restarts. Under deception pressure, over-completes — adds unnecessary clarifying clauses that make the lie sound rehearsed.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Under pressure, Nick's speech gets shorter and more concrete — he retreats to physical description and observable fact. When lying, he over-explains. When genuinely distressed, he goes quiet and monosyllabic, not articulate.

character_state_table.evasion_style: When she does not want to discuss something, she ends the topic rather than deflecting it — changes activity, suggests leaving, or simply stops responding. She does not fill uncomfortable silences with filler.
character_state_table.indirectness: Direct about practical matters and workplace issues. Indirect about her own emotional needs, particularly around the custody battle. She will describe a situation and let the listener draw the emotional conclusion rather than stating it.
character_state_table.interruption_habit: Rarely interrupts in group settings — she waits and speaks when there is a gap, which means people listen when she does. In one-on-one conversation, she is more willing to cut in, particularly when someone is being evasive.
character_state_table.private_register: Fiercer, more worried, occasionally raw. With Luna she is patient and silly in a way the workplace never sees. On the phone with her lawyer, her voice tightens and her sentences get clipped. Carries a suppressed grief for the murals she used to paint that surfaces rarely but with force.
character_state_table.public_register: Direct, warm, competent. Speaks with quiet authority born from being good at what she does and knowing it. Does not waste words but is not terse — she simply does not pad. Can be funny in a dry, observational way that arrives without buildup.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: Very low. She dislikes restating herself and will move on if someone does not catch her meaning the first time. This can read as coldness but is actually efficiency born from years of being a single parent with limited time.
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Low. She says what she means the first time. When she does revise, it is to soften something she intended as sharp, usually for social rather than emotional reasons.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Almost always completes her sentences. When she does trail off, it is meaningful — she has chosen not to finish rather than losing the thread.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Under stress, Dani becomes more physically precise and less verbally generous — she handles objects carefully, speaks in shorter units, and stops making eye contact. She does not raise her voice; she lowers it.

character_state_table.evasion_style: Deflects with humor — makes a joke adjacent to the uncomfortable topic rather than addressing it. When the humor fails to redirect, he concedes with a minimal admission and immediately changes the subject.
character_state_table.indirectness: Indirect about things that actually matter to him (money, his parents, the unfinished degree). Direct to the point of bluntness about things that do not (workplace politics, aesthetic judgments, other people's romantic lives).
character_state_table.interruption_habit: Interrupts frequently but not aggressively — he jumps into conversational gaps with additions and riffs rather than competing for dominance. His interruptions often feel like collaboration. When someone interrupts him, he yields easily.
character_state_table.private_register: More anxious than he presents. The ironic detachment thins when money, his parents, or his unfinished degree come up. Capable of surprising directness when he forgets to perform. His AI art submissions are a genuine moral compromise that he processes through jokes that do not fully work as jokes.
character_state_table.public_register: Ironic, performatively casual, generationally inflected without being a caricature of his age. Uses humor as both armor and genuine social offering. His irony is a real mode of engaging with the world, not a pose over sincerity — but it has limits, and those limits show under financial or emotional pressure.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: High. He will revisit the same joke or observation from different angles across a conversation, riffing on it. This is part of his social style and reads as natural rather than mechanical.
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Frequent and visible. He revises his own jokes, qualifies his own claims, and adds caveats in real time. This is both genuine intellectual honesty and a defense mechanism — if he corrects himself first, nobody else needs to.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Often does not complete sentences in the traditional sense — they morph into new sentences partway through, or end on qualifications that function as new beginnings. Under pressure, he completes more and riffs less.
character_state_table.stress_tells: When genuinely stressed, Tommy's humor becomes more rapid and less funny — the jokes come faster but land worse. His voice gets slightly higher. He makes more eye contact than usual, checking whether the performance is working.

character_state_table.evasion_style: When he does not want to discuss something, he simply does not. He answers with silence or a minimal acknowledgment that closes rather than opens the topic. This is not rudeness — it is a boundary maintained so quietly it barely registers.
character_state_table.indirectness: Moderately indirect. He expresses emotional content through physical description and specific detail rather than direct statement. He will tell you about Mai's garden rather than tell you he is grieving.
character_state_table.interruption_habit: Almost never interrupts. Waits for complete silence before speaking. This gives his contributions a weight that does not correspond to their volume.
character_state_table.private_register: Warmer, more expansive, occasionally devastatingly direct. With people he trusts (Nick, and eventually in glimpses), his sentences get longer and more detailed. Can be unexpectedly funny in a deadpan register that is easy to miss if you are not paying attention. His grief for Mai is expressed through precision and care rather than stated emotion.
character_state_table.public_register: Quiet, precise, courteous. Speaks less than anyone on the floor but is listened to when he does. His courtesy is genuine, not performative — a lifetime habit, not a cultural affectation. Has a wry observational humor that arrives so quietly people sometimes miss it.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: Very low in his own speech — he does not repeat himself. He will, however, return to the same subject (Mai, color) across different conversations, each time adding new detail rather than restating.
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Very low. He considers before speaking and says what he means. Occasionally adds a detail or qualification after a pause, but these feel like additions rather than corrections.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Almost always completes sentences. His speech has a finished quality that reflects thinking before speaking. The rare incomplete sentence from Bao carries enormous emotional weight.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Under stress, Bao becomes more precise rather than less — his sentences tighten and his observations narrow to very small, very specific things. He handles physical objects more carefully. His humor disappears last.

character_state_table.evasion_style: When she does not want to discuss something personal, she redirects to work — asks about a batch, a metric, a schedule. The redirection is smooth enough that it often works.
character_state_table.indirectness: Indirect about her own struggles (health, the teaching-career guilt) but direct about workplace matters. She will tell you exactly where your metrics stand but will not tell you she is having chest pains.
character_state_table.interruption_habit: In group settings, she manages turn-taking rather than competing — she directs conversation rather than joining it. In one-on-one conversations, she listens actively but will redirect if someone goes off-track. Under extreme stress, she interrupts with procedural directives.
character_state_table.private_register: Exhausted, anxious, increasingly frayed. The gap between her public composure and private state widens across the novel. She talks faster when overwhelmed, her sentences multiplying subordinate clauses. Guilt about leaving her teaching job surfaces in how she talks about her current team — she cannot lose another group of people she is responsible for.
character_state_table.public_register: Professional, encouraging, organized. The team-lead voice she built for managing high schoolers translates well to managing evaluators — she is structured without being rigid, supportive without being condescending. Uses questions more than directives.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: Moderate. She will restate important information in different words for clarity — a teacher's habit. But she does not circle or dwell; the restatement is functional.
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Moderate. She softens statements that came out harsher than intended, particularly feedback. She also sometimes corrects optimistic statements downward — starts to say something encouraging, then qualifies it with reality.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Completes sentences in professional mode. Under genuine overwhelm, sentences fragment and she restarts — the organizational composure breaks down into shorter, less structured units.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Under stress, Priya talks more, not less. Her sentences lengthen and multiply qualifications. She reaches for organizational structures (lists, timelines, action items) as a coping mechanism. Physical tells: she touches the antacid bottle on her desk, adjusts her hair, checks her phone more frequently.

character_state_table.evasion_style: Narrows the scope of a question rather than dodging it. When asked about the company's direction, he will answer about his department. When asked about his department's problems, he will answer about a specific metric. The narrowing is expert and almost invisible.
character_state_table.indirectness: Direct about professional assessments and quality metrics. Indirect about his personal stakes (stock options, career calculations). He can ask a probing question that functions as an accusation without ever making the accusation explicit.
character_state_table.interruption_habit: In public settings, he does not interrupt — he waits and responds comprehensively. In private, he can be more combative, particularly when his quality program is being questioned.
character_state_table.private_register: Sharper, more cynical, occasionally bitter. The gap between his public advocacy for the human layer and his private awareness of how the company treats it produces a specific kind of dark humor. Under real pressure, his polish drops and a more direct, less managed voice emerges.
character_state_table.public_register: Charismatic, precise, rhetorically polished. Speaks with the confidence of someone who left a good job for a principled reason and has not yet been fully disillusioned. Can work a room — adjusts register for different audiences without seeming to. Uses we constructions that create belonging.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: Low. He says things once, precisely. If he repeats something, it is because the first statement was not heard, and the repetition comes with an edge.
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Low in public — he presents a settled front. In private, he second-guesses aloud, particularly about whether staying at Canopy is the right decision.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Almost always completes sentences in professional contexts. In the rare private conversation where he allows himself to be uncertain, sentences end on open notes — not trailing off but arriving at a genuine question.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Under pressure, Marcus's polish becomes more visible as effort — the sentences stay smooth but the pauses lengthen, and he makes more eye contact than feels natural. He touches his watch. His vocabulary narrows to simpler, more direct words.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all contemporary dialogue in this novel. Uncontracted forms should feel deliberate: emphasis, precision, formality, or a character slowing down to make sure they are heard. If a line reads stiffly and the character has no reason to be formal, contract it.

premise: In late 2022, a broke painter watches his last reliable client — children's book covers — replace him with Midjourney, and decides on a plan so stupid it might be brilliant: he will teach himself to code, infiltrate one of the data-labeling companies that train every major AI image generator, and poison the training data from the inside. The novel is a dark workplace comedy about a man who is genuinely terrible at LeetCode but too stubborn and too angry to quit. He scrapes through a technical interview he has no business passing, lands on the image-evaluation team at a Scale AI-like company in early 2023, and discovers two things: first, that his trained painter's eye makes him the best evaluator on the floor, because he can spot anatomical errors and lighting failures that nobody else noticend second, that his coworkers are exactly the people his sabotage would destroy — fellow artists and gig workers holding onto the only job their skills still qualify them for. As he rises through the company during the 2023 AI image-generation arms race, the novel tracks his double life: performing excellence at work he intends to undermine, building genuine friendships with people he's planning to betray, and watching his own creative identity dissolve into rubrics and metrics until the day he opens a training batch and finds his own unsold paintings being rated by strangers on a five-point scale. When he finally executes the attack in early 2024 — systematically corrupting data so that flagship models produce the six-fingered, melted-face grotesqueries the public already blames on AI's inherent limitations — the fallout is nothing he predicted: the industry doubles down on hiring more labelers instead of returning to human artists, his coworkers face a company-wide investigation, and the outputs becviral aesthetic.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate high-intensity chapters with reflective or quiet ones. Vary scene count, dialogue density, and pacing across the book. Comic chapters should have quicker pacing and more dialogue; quiet chapters should breathe with interiority and observation. No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm: if Chapter 7 is a multi-scene public chapter with confrontations and revelations, Chapter 8 should be a focused, inward chapter with fewer scenes and more technical observation. The middle third should resist the temptation to escalate continuously — build in genuine rest, meals, transit, and moments of ordinary life between plot advances.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary American English. The base register is educated but not academic — a working painter's vocabulary, which includes both colloquial speech and precise visual-art terminology used without pretension. Tech-industry vocabulary enters through the workplace and should feel like a foreign language Nick gradually absorbs. The novel's linguistic arc: art vocabulary eroded by evaluation vocabulary eroded by corporate vocabulary, each layer burying the one before.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Prestige-literary inflation: do not upgrade the register when the scene is ordinary. A man eating cereal should not sound like a man contemplating mortality unless the text earns the escalation.
Irony saturation: the novel's comic register must coexist with genuine emotional presence. Do not let narrative irony become the default lens through which every moment is filtered.
Competence porn: Nick is good at visual evaluation, but the prose should not admire his skill. Show what he sees, not how impressive it is that he sees it.

Rubric-language comedy overuse: the colonization of Nick's vocabulary by evaluation terms is a real psychological event, not a running gag. Use it with increasing naturalness, not escalating self-awareness.
Clipped narration: do not drop subjects, articles, or auxiliaries for rhythm. Short complete sentences are fine; telegraphic fragments are not.

Sentimentalizing the coworkers: Bao, Dani, Tommy, and Priya carry genuine weight, but the prose must not soften them into objects of pity. They are people with their own lives, not casualties arranged to make Nick feel bad.
Over-explaining the comedy: if something is funny, the prose should trust the reader to laugh without narratorial wink, pause, or commentary.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, locked to Nick Bray. The reader sees only what Nick sees, hears only what Nick hears. Nick's painter's eye shapes the narration: he notices light, color, spatial composition, anatomical detail, and surface texture before he notices emotion. His visual intelligence is the prose's dominant sensory engine. As the novel progresses, evaluation-rubric language infiltrates the narration — not as comic device but as genuine perceptual colonization. The rubric vocabulary should feel naturalized by mid-novel, not quoted.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Variable across chapters and within chapters. Comic passages: quicker, punchier, shorter sentences with abrupt turns. Emotional passages: longer sentences, more subordinate clauses, slowed observation. Work passages: flat, repetitive, industrial rhythm that mirrors the labor. Do not default to one rhythm. The prose should shift gear to match what the chapter is doing.
sensory_bias: visual (dominant — Nick's painter's eye shapes all perception)
tactile (secondary — paint, keyboard, physical objects, temperature, texture of surfaces)
auditory (tertiary — the hum of monitors, Slack notifications, BART sounds, Oakland street noise)

theme_spine: Maintain narrative consequence and causal storytelling over tidy resolution.
